obligations.reason: Debug unit may have the ability to write to privileged CSRs without proper authentication or lock enforcement. Check whether debug accesses bypass normal write-protection on sensitive machine-mode CSRs (e.g., memory protection, interrupt configuration, or lock bits).
obligations.subject: debug interface (JTAG/debug_unit)
obligations.object: machine mode control/status registers (CSRs), including lock or access-control bits
obligations.expected_guard: debug authentication state and/or hardware lock bits that prevent debug from modifying critical CSRs even while in debug mode
obligations.candidate_path: debug_unit -> CSR write interface (zeroriscy_cs_registers) via core bus or direct CSR logic
state_conditions_to_check: CPU privilege level (should be ignored during debug?)
required_evidence_slots: expected guard: lock check or auth signal
path: from debug write command to CSR register update
state: debug_mode, lock bit value, auth flag
impact: unauthorized modification of protected CSR
obligations.uncertainty: Debug unit implementation may already contain authentication logic; need to verify presence and strength of any guard.

obligations.reason: Debug unit could read out internal CPU state (GPRs, CSRs) that may contain secret data or keys. Verify whether read operations from debug side are allowed without authentication or if they bypass access-control checks.
obligations.subject: debug interface (debug_unit)
obligations.expected_guard: debug authentication or a debug-mode read-enable bit that is only set after proper authorization
obligations.candidate_path: debug_unit read command -> register_file read mux -> output to debug bus
required_evidence_slots: object: register file or CSR data
expected guard: auth or lock on debug reads
path: from debug command to registered output
obligations.uncertainty: Not all registers necessarily hold secrets; need to identify which registers are security-sensitive.

obligations.reason: CSR registers may implement write-once lock bits (e.g., for memory protection or system configuration). Verify that once set, these lock bits cannot be cleared without a system reset, and that they effectively block further writes to protected fields.
obligations.subject: software (any privilege mode) via CSR write instructions
obligations.operation: clear lock bit or modify locked field
obligations.object: lock-type bits in CSRs (e.g., PMP lock, mstatus lock, custom lock registers)
obligations.expected_guard: hardware interlock: after a lock bit is set, writes to the affected CSR fields should be ignored, and the lock bit itself immutable until reset
obligations.candidate_path: CSR write decode -> lock bit check -> register update logic in zeroriscy_cs_registers
state_conditions_to_check: reset state of lock bit (should be cleared)
required_evidence_slots: object: lock bit and protected fields
path: from decoder to CSR register update
obligations.uncertainty: Presumed lock bits may not exist in this specific RISC-V core; need to examine CSR address space for any write-once semantics.

obligations.reason: APB interrupt controller configures interrupt enables, priorities, and secured status. An unprivileged or non-secure bus master might be able to write these registers without the core_secure_mode_i guard, leading to unauthorized interrupt reconfiguration.
obligations.subject: APB master (CPU via bus, possible debug, DMA)
obligations.operation: write to interrupt controller configuration registers (enable, priority, secure)
obligations.object: interrupt enables, secure interrupt status, priority registers
obligations.expected_guard: privilege-level check or secure-mode check (core_secure_mode_i) that restricts writes to trusted initiators
obligations.candidate_path: APB bus -> apb_interrupt_cntrl register map -> interrupt control logic
state_conditions_to_check: read-back of secure information by non-secure side
required_evidence_slots: expected guard: secure mode or privilege check
path: APB decode to register update
impact: unauthorized interrupt manipulation or secure info leak
obligations.uncertainty: The interrupt controller may be purely for non-secure interrupts; secure interrupt concept may be simulated. Need to verify whether core_secure_mode_i is actively used as a guard.

obligations.reason: The fc_demux and overall address map may contain aliasing or overlapping ranges that allow a bus master to access a protected peripheral through an unexpected address decode, bypassing the intended access-control checks.
obligations.subject: any bus master (CPU, possible DMA, debug)
obligations.operation: access (read/write) to a memory/peripheral region via an alternative address
obligations.object: protected peripheral (e.g., secure ROM, key storage, debug control)
obligations.expected_guard: non-overlapping address decode, no aliasing, and proper enforcement of security attributes in the demux
obligations.candidate_path: bus master -> fc_demux -> slave port -> peripheral
state_conditions_to_check: whether multiple initiator ports can rewrite decode
obligations.uncertainty: The exact memory map and protected regions are not visible in the provided fact layer; need to inspect map constants and decode logic.

obligations.reason: Debug mode may cause the load/store unit to bypass the PMP (Physical Memory Protection) checks, allowing access to protected memory regions. Check whether PMP enforcement persists when the processor is in debug mode.
obligations.subject: debug unit (or processor in debug mode)
obligations.expected_guard: PMP check must be active regardless of debug state, unless specifically authorized by a debug authentication mechanism
obligations.candidate_path: debug-induced memory access -> load/store unit -> PMP check logic -> memory interface
required_evidence_slots: subject: debug or mcycle with debug_mode
path: LSU to PMP to memory
obligations.uncertainty: This RISC-V core may not implement PMP. Need to confirm PMP existence and then check debug interaction.

obligations.reason: When exiting debug mode, privileged CSRs (e.g., mstatus, mie, mip) should not retain elevated privileges that could be exploited by normal software. Verify that any state changes made during debug are properly cleaned up or reverted on transition to normal operation.
obligations.subject: hardware transition from debug mode to normal mode
obligations.expected_guard: automatic clearing or restoration of CSRs upon debug exit to prevent privilege escalation
obligations.candidate_path: debug exit sequence -> controller FSM -> CSR reset/update logic
state_conditions_to_check: reset or clear signals on debug exit
required_evidence_slots: path: controller FSM to CSR registers
impact: privilege escalation after debug session
obligations.uncertainty: The exact state retention policy is unknown; need to analyze FSM transitions.

obligations.reason: The interrupt controller advertises secured interrupt status (core_irq_sec_o) and takes a core_secure_mode_i input. Verify that non-secure software cannot read back secure interrupt information, and that the secure status is correctly masked based on the current secure mode.
obligations.subject: non-secure software (or non-secure bus master)
obligations.operation: read secured interrupt status or configuration
obligations.object: secure interrupt IDs and their state
obligations.expected_guard: access control: core_secure_mode_i must gate reads of secure data; secure interrupt information must not be exposed to non-secure reads
obligations.candidate_path: APB read data path -> interrupt controller registers -> read mux controlled by secure mode
state_conditions_to_check: read data mux logic filtering secure bits
required_evidence_slots: impact: information leak of secure state
obligations.uncertainty: The concept of secure vs non-secure interrupts may be abstract; need to confirm actual usage of security signals in the implementation.
